message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "BEST LOCAL / OPEN-SOURCE TEXT-TO-SPEECH (TTS) MODELS TO REPLACE OR AUGMENT KOKORO for an English educational-podcast pipeline, as of 2026. Baseline to beat: Kokoro-82M (hexgrad/Kokoro-82M), currently run locally on Apple Silicon (CPU + MPS), producing 24kHz audio, multiple voices (af_heart/am_onyx/bf_isabella/bm_george etc.), American + British English, two-voice format (narrator + question voice). Evaluate candidates across these angles: (1) QUALITY/NATURALNESS — which open/local TTS models beat Kokoro on naturalness and intelligibility for English (candidates to assess: XTTS-v2/Coqui, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Fish-Speech/OpenAudio, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Chatterbox, Kitten-TTS, Zonos, and any newer 2025-2026 open models) — report MOS/quality benchmarks, license (commercial/redistribution OK?), and model size. (2) WORD/PHONEME-LEVEL TIMING & ALIGNMENT — which models natively output word/phoneme timestamps (durations), and what forced-alignment tools (Montreal Forced Aligner, WhisperX, aeneas, ctc-forced-aligner) best produce a text↔audio timing map from existing audio — this enables content-aware speed compression, script-highlighting, and a true text-to-audio map. (3) HIGH-SPEED COMPREHENSIBILITY — which model's output stays most intelligible under aggressive time-compression / at high playback speed (crisp consonants, clean formant transitions); also the best FORMANT/robotic option (eSpeak-NG, RHVoice, DECtalk-like) for an extreme 16x 'ultra' track. (4) MULTI-VOICE & VOICE CONTROL — voice variety, voice cloning, prosody/rate control, and ease of adding new voices for a narrator+question two-speaker format. (5) LOCAL-INFERENCE PRACTICALITY on Apple Silicon and CPU — real-time factor / batch render speed, RAM/VRAM needs, MPS/CoreML/ONNX support, latency, and integration effort to swap into a Python pipeline that today calls Kokoro. For EACH recommended model give: quality vs Kokoro, license, local-inference feasibility on a Mac, whether it emits alignment, and a clear verdict on whether it's worth switching to (or adding alongside Kokoro). Prefer authoritative/benchmark sources and recent (2025-2026) comparisons; flag where claims are vendor marketing vs independent evaluation."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://www.tryspeakeasy.io/blog/open-source-text-to-speech-2026
**Title:** Best Open Source TTS Models in 2026: Kokoro, Chatterbox, Fish Audio Compared
**Found via:** voice control — cloning, prosody, multi-speaker search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- agents-sdk: Build AI agents on Cloudflare Workers using the Agents SDK. Load when creating stateful agents, durable workflows, real-time WebSocket apps, scheduled tasks, MCP servers, chat applications, voice agents, or browser automation. Covers Agent class, state management, callable RPC, Workflows, durable execution, queues, retries, observability, and React hooks. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- cloudflare: Comprehensive Cloudflare platform skill covering Workers, Pages, storage (KV, D1, R2), AI (Workers AI, Vectorize, Agents SDK), feature flags (Flagship), networking (Tunnel, Spectrum), security (WAF, DDoS), and infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Pulumi). Use for any Cloudflare development task. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare-email-service: Send and receive transactional emails with Cloudflare Email Service (Email Sending + Email Routing). Use when building email sending (Workers binding or REST API), email routing, Agents SDK email handling, or integrating email into any app — Workers, Node.js, Python, Go, etc. Also use for email deliverability, SPF/DKIM/DMARC, wrangler email setup, MCP email tools, or when a coding agent needs to send emails. Even for simple requests like "add email to my Worker" — this skill has critical config details.
- cloudflare-one: Guides Cloudflare One Zero Trust and SASE work across Access, Gateway, WARP, Tunnel, Cloudflare WAN, DLP, CASB, device posture, and identity. Use when designing, configuring, troubleshooting, or reviewing Cloudflare One deployments. Retrieval-first: use current Cloudflare docs/API schemas instead of embedded product docs.
- cloudflare-one-migrations: Plans migrations from Zscaler ZIA/ZPA, Palo Alto, legacy VPN, SWG, or SASE stacks to Cloudflare One. Use for migration assessments, policy mapping, rollout plans, and parity/gap analysis.
- durable-objects: Create and review Cloudflare Durable Objects. Use when building stateful coordination (chat rooms, multiplayer games, booking systems), implementing RPC methods, SQLite storage, alarms, WebSockets, or reviewing DO code for best practices. Covers Workers integration, wrangler config, and testing with Vitest. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- sandbox-sdk: Build sandboxed applications for secure code execution. Load when building AI code execution, code interpreters, CI/CD systems, interactive dev environments, or executing untrusted code. Covers Sandbox SDK lifecycle, commands, files, code interpreter, and preview URLs. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- turnstile-spin: Set up Cloudflare Turnstile end-to-end in a project — scan the codebase, create the widget via the Cloudflare API, deploy the managed siteverify Worker, write the frontend snippets, validate, and persist the skill. Load this when a user asks to add Turnstile, set up CAPTCHA, protect a form from bots, or fix a Turnstile integration. Mirrors developers.cloudflare.com/turnstile/spin.
- web-perf: Analyzes web performance using Chrome DevTools MCP. Measures Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) and supplementary metrics (FCP, TBT, Speed Index), identifies render-blocking resources, network dependency chains, layout shifts, caching issues, and accessibility gaps. Use when asked to audit, profile, debug, or optimize page load performance, Lighthouse scores, or site speed. Biases towards retrieval from current documentation over pre-trained knowledge.
- workers-best-practices: Reviews and authors Cloudflare Workers code against production best practices. Load when writing new Workers, reviewing Worker code, configuring wrangler.jsonc, or checking for common Workers anti-patterns (streaming, floating promises, global state, secrets, bindings, observability). Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- wrangler: Cloudflare Workers CLI for deploying, developing, and managing Workers, KV, R2, D1, Vectorize, Hyperdrive, Workers AI, Containers, Queues, Workflows, Pipelines, and Secrets Store. Load before running wrangler commands to ensure correct syntax and best practices. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the page content and extract the relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract all claims about open-source/local TTS models (Kokoro, Chatterbox, Fish Audio/Fish-Speech, XTTS, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Parler, MeloTTS, Orpheus, Zonos, Kitten-TTS, etc.). For each model report: quality/naturalness claims, MOS or benchmark numbers, license (commercial/redistribution), model size, voice cloning/multi-voice capabilities, prosody/rate control, local inference feasibility (Apple Silicon/CPU/GPU, RAM/VRAM, real-time factor), and any word/phoneme timestamp/alignment support. Also report: the publish date of the article, the author, whether this is independent benchmark/evaluation or vendor marketing/blog. Quote exact sentences where possible.

content.content:
# Open Source TTS Claims Extraction

**Article Metadata:**
- Published: April 14, 2026
- Author: SpeakEasy Team
- Type: Vendor blog (SpeakEasy promotes their own API)

---

## Models Analyzed

### Kokoro
**Quality Claims:**
- "210x real-time speed on an RTX 4090, 36x on a free T4 in Colab"
- No independent benchmarks cited; quality assumed adequate for "most listeners"

**Specifications:**
- Model size: 82M parameters
- License: Apache 2.0 (commercial use permitted)
- Voice cloning: None; "54 preset voices, no voice cloning"
- Prosody control: Not mentioned
- Local inference: "runs on basically anything — gaming laptops, free cloud GPUs, even CPUs"
- Timestamps: Not mentioned
- VRAM: 2-3GB

---

### Chatterbox (Resemble AI)
**Quality Claims:**
- "In blind tests, Chatterbox hit a 63.75% preference rate against ElevenLabs"
- No source cited for this benchmark

**Specifications:**
- Model size: 350M (Turbo variant)
- License: MIT
- Voice cloning: "5-10 second audio sample"
- Prosody: "Emotion exaggeration control — dial expressiveness up or down"
- Paralinguistic features: "native paralinguistic tags (`[laugh]`, `[cough]`, `[chuckle]`)"
- Latency: "sub-200ms latency"
- VRAM: 4-8GB
- Language: English only
- Local inference: Feasible (Turbo variant specified)
- Timestamps: Not mentioned

---

### VibeVoice (Microsoft)
**Quality Claims:**
- "generate up to 90 minutes of continuous, multi-speaker audio"
- No MOS or preference scores provided

**Specifications:**
- Model size: 1.5B (0.5B real-time variant available)
- License: Research-grade with watermarks
- Voice cloning: Not supported
- Multi-speaker: 4 distinct speakers with "stable voice identities"
- Latency: "~300ms first audio" (0.5B variant)
- Languages: English and Chinese only
- Local inference: Feasible but infrastructure needs not detailed
- Timestamps: Not mentioned

---

### Dia2 (Nari Labs)
**Quality Claims:**
- No quantitative benchmarks provided

**Specifications:**
- Model size: 1B-2B variants
- License: Apache 2.0
- Voice cloning: "from an audio sample for consistent characters"
- Architecture: "Streaming architecture — starts generating audio before the full text"
- Language: English only
- Prosody: Support for nonverbal tags "(laughs)" but noted as "unpredictable"
- Local inference: Feasible (variants exist)
- Timestamps: Not mentioned

---

### Qwen3-TTS
**Quality Claims:**
- "97ms streaming latency" (not a quality metric)
- No comparative benchmarks

**Specifications:**
- Model size: 600M parameters
- License: Apache 2.0
- Voice cloning: "zero-shot voice cloning from just 3 seconds of audio"
- Languages: 10 languages
- Latency: 97ms streaming (GPU-dependent)
- VRAM: ~4GB (GPU required for target latency)
- Local inference: "Needs a GPU for that 97ms latency. CPU performance is slower"
- Timestamps: Not mentioned

---

### Fish Audio S2 Pro
**Quality Claims:**
- "currently sits at the top of independent TTS benchmarks"
- "81.88% win rate" on EmergentTTS-Eval
- "beating ElevenLabs, Seed-TTS, and offerings from Google and OpenAI"
- No source link provided for benchmarks

**Specifications:**
- Model size: ~4.4B parameters
- License: "Open weights, not fully open source. Commercial use...requires a paid license"
- Voice cloning: "Voice cloning across 80+ languages from a short reference sample"
- Prosody: "free-form emotion control — embed natural-language instructions"
- Latency: "~100ms time-to-first-audio on H200 GPU"
- Local inference: Feasible on high-end GPUs; caveat: "mileage will vary"
- Timestamps: Not mentioned

---

## Notable Omissions
- XTTS, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Parler, MeloTTS, and other models mentioned in query were **not reviewed** in this article
- No word/phoneme timestamp or alignment support mentioned for any model
- No Apple Silicon or CPU-only inference claims (only CPU feasibility for Kokoro)

content.text: Based on the fetched content, I'll extract the relevant claims.

claims.claim: Kokoro-82M is Apache 2.0 licensed (commercial use permitted), has 82M parameters, runs at 210x real-time on an RTX 4090 and even on CPUs, needs 2-3GB VRAM, and offers 54 preset voices with no voice cloning.
claims.quote: 210x real-time speed on an RTX 4090, 36x on a free T4 in Colab ... runs on basically anything — gaming laptops, free cloud GPUs, even CPUs ... 54 preset voices, no voice cloning

claims.claim: Chatterbox (Resemble AI) achieved a 63.75% blind-test preference rate against ElevenLabs, is MIT licensed, supports voice cloning from a 5-10 second sample, has emotion-exaggeration control, ~350M params (Turbo), sub-200ms latency, 4-8GB VRAM, English only.
claims.quote: In blind tests, Chatterbox hit a 63.75% preference rate against ElevenLabs ... Voice cloning: "5-10 second audio sample" ... Emotion exaggeration control — dial expressiveness up or down ... sub-200ms latency

claims.claim: Fish Audio S2 Pro tops independent TTS benchmarks with an 81.88% win rate on EmergentTTS-Eval (beating ElevenLabs, Seed-TTS, Google, OpenAI), but is open-weights (not fully open source) and requires a paid license for commercial use; ~4.4B params, voice cloning across 80+ languages, free-form emotion control.
claims.quote: currently sits at the top of independent TTS benchmarks ... 81.88% win rate on EmergentTTS-Eval ... beating ElevenLabs, Seed-TTS, and offerings from Google and OpenAI ... Open weights, not fully open source. Commercial use...requires a paid license

claims.claim: Newer 2025-2026 open models including Microsoft VibeVoice (1.5B, multi-speaker up to 90 min, research license with watermarks, English/Chinese), Nari Labs Dia2 (Apache 2.0, streaming, voice cloning), and Qwen3-TTS (600M, Apache 2.0, 3-second zero-shot cloning, 10 languages, needs GPU) are positioned as Kokoro alternatives.
claims.quote: VibeVoice (Microsoft) ... generate up to 90 minutes of continuous, multi-speaker audio ... Research-grade with watermarks ... Qwen3-TTS ... zero-shot voice cloning from just 3 seconds of audio ... Needs a GPU for that 97ms latency. CPU performance is slower

claims.claim: None of the models reviewed in this article are reported to provide word/phoneme-level timestamps or alignment output, and no model is benchmarked specifically for Apple Silicon (MPS/CoreML) inference.
claims.quote: No word/phoneme timestamp or alignment support mentioned for any model ... No Apple Silicon or CPU-only inference claims (only CPU feasibility for Kokoro)

content.text:
I've extracted the key claims from the SpeakEasy article. Summary of my assessment:

**Source quality: blog (vendor blog).** Published April 14, 2026 by the "SpeakEasy Team." SpeakEasy promotes its own TTS API, so this is vendor marketing rather than independent evaluation. Critically, several headline benchmark numbers (Chatterbox's 63.75% preference rate, Fish Audio's 81.88% EmergentTTS-Eval win rate) are cited **without source links**, so they should be treated as unverified.

**Relevance to the research question:** Moderate. The article directly covers the Kokoro baseline and several alternatives, but it has significant gaps relative to the research angles:
- It does **not** review XTTS-v2, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus, or Kitten-TTS — many of the candidate models named in the question.
- It provides **no** word/phoneme timestamp or forced-alignment information (research angle 2) for any model.
- It provides **no** Apple Silicon / MPS / CoreML inference data (research angle 5) — only a generic "even CPUs" note for Kokoro. Most latency figures are GPU-bound (RTX 4090, H200, T4).
- It says nothing about high-speed comprehensibility or formant/robotic options (research angle 3).

The most load-bearing takeaways are the licensing and capability facts: Kokoro (Apache 2.0, 82M, no cloning), Chatterbox (MIT, cloning + emotion control — a strong open candidate), and Fish Audio S2 Pro (top benchmarks but **paid commercial license**, ~4.4B params — likely too heavy/restrictive for the local Mac pipeline). These should be cross-checked against independent benchmarks given the vendor-blog nature of the source.
